question.text: Under SEBI's guidelines on unclaimed redemption and dividend amounts, if the amount remains unclaimed for more than 3 years, the investor upon claiming will receive payment at ________.

answer.text: The NAV prevailing at the end of the 3rd year from the due date
The latest prevailing NAV at the time of claim
The face value of the units
The lower of the NAV at 3 years or the current NAV

explanation.text:
**The Logic:** SEBI's framework for unclaimed amounts works in two phases: (1) Within 3 years of the due date, if the investor claims, they receive payment at the prevailing NAV at the time of claim. (2) After 3 years, payment is fixed at the NAV prevailing at the end of the 3rd year from the due date. The unclaimed amounts are invested in a separate plan (typically a money market plan), and any gains from this investment accrue to the AMC, not the investor, after the 3-year mark.
**The Trap:** "Latest prevailing NAV" (Option B) is correct only for claims within 3 years, not after. "Face value" (Option C) ignores market appreciation/depreciation entirely. "Lower of two NAVs" (Option D) is a fabricated formula. The critical distinction is the 3-year cutoff: before 3 years = current NAV, after 3 years = NAV at end of year 3.
**Cross-Exam Context:** Unclaimed amounts treatment is tested to assess knowledge of investor protection mechanisms. The AMC must make genuine efforts to contact investors before amounts become unclaimed, and must maintain a separate plan for these amounts.
**Key Takeaway:** Unclaimed amounts: within 3 years = prevailing NAV at claim date. After 3 years = NAV at end of 3rd year (frozen). Claim early to get current NAV.
